rulings.Case Title: PATRICIA SOLOMUA VS. SAN FRANCISCO GENERAL HOSPITAL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant William Schecter, M.D.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 31, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT WILLIAM SCHECTER, M.D's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant William Schecter, M.D.'s Motion For Summary Judgment Defendant William Schecter's motion for summary judgement on the single cause of for medical malpractice in the complaint filed by plaintiff Patricia Solumua is granted. The undisputed facts show that Ms. Solumua's claim was time-barred no later than 2014, three years before this lawsuit was filed. The undisputed evidence is that Ms. Solumua consulted with attorneys about suing Dr. Schecter regarding the 1998 surgery up to and including 2013. Therefore, giving Ms. Solumua every benefit of the doubt and disregarding the undisputed facts suggesting that Ms. Solumua's claim was likely time-barred long before then, Ms. Solumua was required to sue Dr. Schecter no later than the end of 2014. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Dr. Schecter is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: CALIFORNIA BUSINESS INTERIORS, INC. VS. KNOLL, INC., DBA NORTH AMERICA KNOLL, INC., A ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings. (Added Per Ex Parte Order Of 10/22/18)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 31, 2018, Line 10, Defendant'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings. (Added Per Ex Parte Order Of 10/22/18) Defendants Knoll, Inc. and Jennyfer Aguilera's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to all three causes of action alleged in the complaint filed by plaintiff California Business Interiors, Inc. is granted without leave to amend as to all three causes of action. California law requires that CBI allege that defendants committed independently wrongful conduct in allegedly requiring CBI to sell Hogue to KBM. CBI has not done so nor does it appear that it can do so. CBI improperly relies on Lowell v. Mother's Cake & Cookie Co. (1978) 79 Cal. App. 3d 13 to support its position that defendants' threat of not renewing CBI's contract with Knoll is actionable. Though the California Supreme Court has not explicitly identified the discussion in Lowell regarding the tort of interference with prospective economic advantage as disapproved, decisions of the California Supreme Court regarding that tort beginning with the seminal case of Della Penna v. Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc. (1995) 11 Cal. 4th 376 make plain that the discussion of the elements of the tort in Lowell is no longer good law. Most notably, the statements in Lowell that "even if the means used by the defendant are entirely lawful, intentional interference with prospective advantage constitutes actionable wrong" and a defendant has the burden of pleading and justification and privilege to avoid liability are irreconcilable with the pleading requirements of the tort enunciated by the California Supreme Court in Della Penna and repeatedly re-affirmed thereafter. The allegations in Lowell that were held to withstand a demurrer in 1978 do not survive a pleading challenge in 2018. The allegations of the complaint show that the central allegation of interference, coercion and illegality - defendants' threat not to renew the contract - is the only causative component of CBI's alleged requirement to sell Hogue to KBM. Since defendants had the contractual right not to renew the contract, CBI has not and it appears that it cannot state an actionable claim. None of the other allegations of misconduct by defendants factually alleged or logically caused CBI to sell Hogue to KBM as opposed to some other prospective buyer and there has been no showing that CBI can amend around this deficiency. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: JYOTHSNA TALKAD ET AL VS. 1314 POLK STREET OWNERS ASSOCIATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Dipali Shah Dipali Shah'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Request For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions (Transferred From Dept. 501 On 10-19-18)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 31, 2018, Line 3, Defendant Dipali Shah's Motion To Compel Responses To Request For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions (Transferred From Dept. 501 On 10-19-18) Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted as follow: Plaintiff, Radhakrshna Talkad is to provide full and complete responses and documents with 20-days. Objections are not waived. Sanctions in the total amount of $1,580 for the three motions on calendar shall be paid to Defendant within 10-days. Compliance shall be based on the date of Notice of Entry of this Order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@lowball.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ROCKEFELLER CAPITAL MANAGEMENT, LLC A DELAWARE VS. FOCUS FINANCIAL PARTNERS LLC A DELAWARE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Christopher Dupuy, Rockefeller Capital Management, L.P., Rockefeller Financial Llc, And Rockefeller & Co. Llc'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, October 31, 2018, Line 8, Part 1 of 2 tentative rulings: Plaintiffs Christopher Dupuy, Rockefeller Capital Management, L.P., Rockefeller Financial Llc, And Rockefeller & Co. Llc'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction Plaintiffs Rockefeller Capital Management L.P., Rockefeller & Co. LLC, Rockefeller Financial LLC, and Christopher Dupuy's motion for preliminary injunction is granted in substantial part. Until there is a final determination on plaintiffs' request for injunctive relief or an order of this court to the contrary, defendant Focus Financial Partners LLC and its officers, agents, employees, representatives, and all persons acting in concert or participating with them are restrained and enjoined from directly or indirectly seeking to enforce its non-competition and non-solicitation restrictive covenants as to Mr. Dupuy in California. This order does not preclude Focus from continuing to litigate in Delaware nor does it preclude Focus from seeking to protect what it asserts as trade secrets or other protected information under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, the Defend Trade Secrets Act, or other laws. Mr. Dupuy may work for the Rockefeller entities in California provided that in doing so Mr. Dupuy and the Rockefeller entities are not in violation of the August 28, 2018 order from the Delaware Chancery Court prohibiting the use of Focus' Confidential Information. The sole forum selection clause in the Dupuy/Focus agreements is in the November 22, 2017 agreement and that clause is invalid per Labor Code 925 based on the record which supports plaintiffs' position that Mr. Dupuy's receipt of units per that agreement was compensation for his employment. Although there is no case on point, this court interprets the phrase "condition of employment" in section 925 as including any requirement to receive employment compensation. The choice of law provisions in the Dupuy/Focus agreements are invalid per Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court (1992) 3 Cal. 4th 459 because there is a conflict between California and Delaware law regarding the enforceability of the restrictive covenants and California has a materially greater interest than Delaware regarding the enforceability of the restrictive covenants. As an alternative holding, the sole forum selection clause and the choice of law provisions are invalid per Verdugo v. Alliantgroup, L.P. (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 141 because Business and Professions Code 16600 is an unwaivable right for a California employee and Focus contests the application of that statute in Delaware. Plaintiffs have demonstrated a likelihood of prevailing on the merits that the restrictive covenants are unenforceable per section 16600. Plaintiffs have also demonstrated irreparable harm as it would be difficult to quantify their lost business and employment opportunities. Nor has Focus shown that it would suffer any harm by the granting of an injunction allowing Mr. Dupuy to work for the Rockefeller entities in California in compliance with the Delaware court's August 28, 2018 order. All other injunctive relief sought by plaintiffs, including the request to maintain the status quo relating to Mr. Dupuy's shares, units and compensation, is denied based on a failure by plaintiffs to show that they are likely to obtain such relief at trial and/or the failure to grant such relief at this time would cause them irreparable harm.
